subject: Mathematics
topic: Plan, Build, Grow — Measurement, Area, Volume, and Data

lesson_overview.overview: Students act as a junior school grounds team across 10 project weeks. They measure planter box dimensions, calculate area and volume, estimate material quantities, record and graph plant growth data, and use their data to make a justified recommendation. Math 8 outcomes focus on measurement in context, area and volume calculations, data collection and graphing, and simple ratio reasoning. Weeks 4 and 8 are built-in flex weeks for pacing.
lesson_overview.essential_question: How can measuring, calculating, and graphing help us make better decisions about our planter box?
lesson_overview.big_idea: Mathematics gives us tools to compare options, estimate costs, track change over time, and support our decisions with evidence.

sequence.focus: Identify what to measure. Label dimensions on a diagram. Review length, width, and height.
Measure or read three planter designs. Record dimensions in a comparison table.
Calculate the area of the planting surface. Compare planter designs by area.
Flex week. Ruler review, area catch-up, or problem practice.
Calculate volume. Estimate how many bags of soil each planter needs.
Tally materials needed. Organize quantities in a table. Review units.
Record plant growth measurements. Begin a line graph.
Flex week. Extend graph, review data accuracy, or catch up.
Analyze growth trends. Describe what the graph shows.
Use calculations and data to make a justified recommendation.

lesson_overview.differentiation_note: Three support levels are embedded throughout. Level A: pre-labeled diagrams, fill-in calculations with steps provided, graphing on pre-plotted axes. Level B: formula reminders, partially completed tables, labeled graph templates. Level C: open-format recording, multi-step problems, extension comparisons.
integrity_checks: All calculations are set in the real context of the planter project.
Formula reminders are visible on student materials — memorization is not the goal.
Measurement units are explicitly labeled in every task.
Flex weeks give time to revisit area or volume before moving to data.
Final recommendation must reference at least one number from the project.

teacher_guide.big_idea: Math 8 students are measuring and calculating in a real context: the planter box is their manipulative, their data set, and their real-world application at the same time.
learning_goals: Identify and measure length, width, and height of a rectangular prism.
Calculate the area of a rectangle using A = L × W.
Calculate the volume of a rectangular prism using V = L × W × H.
Collect, organize, and graph data in a line graph.
Use measurements and graph data to support a simple recommendation.
materials: Planter box dimension cards (three design options)
Area and volume formula reference cards
Graph paper or pre-printed graph templates
Growth log table and graphing sheet (Week 7–9 graphic organizers)
Final recommendation card (Week 10 final response sheet)

timing.activity: Settle, agenda, quick review of last week's measurement concept.
Direct instruction and worked example. Teacher models calculation or graph step on board.
Guided practice — class completes one example together before independent work.
Supported work time. Teacher circulates. Level A students may receive pre-labeled templates.
Teacher checkpoint or small-group problem solving.
Sharing: one student reads their answer aloud. Class checks.

teacher_notes: Always model the full calculation before students attempt it.
Units matter every time. Circle or underline units in every answer during modeling.
Calculators are available throughout — the math goal is setting up the problem, not mental arithmetic.
For graphing: pre-plot axis labels with students before any data is entered.
Flex weeks can be used to re-run area or volume if students struggled.
If physical measurements aren't available, use the dimension cards provided.
The final recommendation must include at least one number — encourage students to quote their own data.

week_4: Redo area calculations with new dimensions
week_8: Add more data points to the growth graph
Review how to read and describe a trend line
Revisit volume if soil estimates were unclear
Catch up missing growth log entries

days.carryover_note: Labeled diagram from Week 1 is used as a reference in Weeks 2–5.

content.subtitle: This week: identify what to measure and label the dimensions of a planter box.

slides.title: What do we need to know?

content.scenario: Before we can build a planter box, we need measurements. We need to know how long, how wide, and how tall it should be.
prompts: What does length mean?
What does width mean?
What does height mean?

rows: Length (L) — the longest horizontal side of a shape
Width (W) — the shorter horizontal side of a shape
Height (H) — how tall the shape is, measured vertically
centimetre (cm) — for small measurements, like a pencil
metre (m) — for larger measurements, like a planter box
millimetre (mm) — for very precise measurements
1 metre = 100 centimetres
Always write the unit next to your number: e.g., 45 cm, not just 45

prompts: The length of a planter box is the ___ side.
We measure height using a ___ .
If a planter is 60 cm long and 30 cm wide, the ___ is longer.

task_sheet.title: Week 1 Task Sheet — Label the Dimensions
instructions: Study the planter box diagram on your sheet.
Label the length, width, and height using the arrows shown.
Answer the questions below using the correct units.

tasks.label: Part 1: Label the diagram
tasks.prompt:
Use the word bank to label the three arrows on the planter box diagram.

Word bank: Length (L)  Width (W)  Height (H)

render_hints.heading: Planter Box Diagram
render_hints.help: Level A: arrows are pre-labelled — check each one. Level B: match each arrow to the word bank. Level C: label without the word bank.

tasks.label: Part 2: Read and record
tasks.prompt:
Use the dimension card for Planter A to answer these questions:

Length of Planter A = ________ cm
Width of Planter A = ________ cm
Height of Planter A = ________ cm
Which dimension is the largest? ________

render_hints.help: Remember to write the unit (cm) after every number.

tasks.label: Part 3: Explain
tasks.prompt:
Why does it matter that we know all three dimensions before we build?

Level B: Knowing the ___, ___, and ___ helps us because ___.
Level C: Write your own answer in one or two sentences.

render_hints.help: Think: what would happen if we only knew one dimension?

embedded_supports: Word bank: length, width, height, centimetres, metres, measure
Sentence frame: The three dimensions are ___, ___, and ___.
Level A: dimension labels are printed on the diagram for reference.
success_criteria: I can label length, width, and height on a diagram.
I can read a measurement and write it with the correct unit.
I can explain why we need all three dimensions.

exit_ticket.title: Week 1 Exit Check
exit_ticket.prompt: A planter box is 80 cm long, 40 cm wide, and 25 cm tall. Write: L = ___ W = ___ H = ___. Which dimension is the smallest?
success_criteria: I wrote all three dimensions with units.
I identified the smallest dimension.

days.carryover_note: Dimension table from Week 2 is used for area calculations in Week 3.

content.subtitle: This week: read dimensions for three planter options and record them in a comparison table.

prompts: What does L stand for?
What unit are we using this project?
Which dimension tells us how tall the planter is?

rows: A comparison table has rows (across) and columns (down).
Each row is one planter option. Each column is one measurement.
Read the dimension card, find the right cell, and record the number with its unit.
greater than (>) — one value is larger
less than (<) — one value is smaller
equal to (=) — two values are the same
difference — how far apart two values are (subtract)
largest / smallest — comparing all options

prompts: Without calculating, which planter looks biggest to you?
How could we check which planter is actually the largest?
What information do we need to compare them fairly?

graphic_organizer.title: Planter Comparison Table
rows: Length (cm)
Width (cm)
Height (cm)
Which is the longest?
Which is the widest?
Which is the tallest?
graphic_organizer.prompt: Use the three dimension cards to complete the table. Record all measurements with units.
success_criteria: I recorded all three dimensions for each planter.
I wrote the correct units after every number.
I can identify which planter has the largest dimension for each row.

exit_ticket.title: Week 2 Exit Check
exit_ticket.prompt: Circle the correct answer: Planter B is 90 cm long. Planter C is 75 cm long. Planter ___ is longer. The difference is ___ cm.
success_criteria: I identified which planter is longer.
I calculated the difference correctly.

days.carryover_note: Area calculations from Week 3 feed into soil estimation in Week 5.

content.subtitle: This week: use the formula A = L × W to find the planting surface area of each planter.

content.task: Use your comparison table from Week 2.
prompts: What were the dimensions of Planter A?
Which planter was the widest?
What formula do we use to find area?

rows: Area = Length × Width
Area is measured in square units: cm² or m²
Area tells us how much planting surface the planter has.
Planter A: L = 80 cm, W = 40 cm
A = 3200 cm²
This means Planter A has 3200 square centimetres of planting surface.

prompts: A planter with more area can hold ___ plants.
Which of our planters has the most planting surface?
If the client wants to grow many plants, which planter should we recommend?

task_sheet.title: Week 3 Task Sheet — Area Calculations
instructions: Use the formula A = L × W.
Show your work in the calculation box.
Write your answer with units (cm²).

tasks.label: Part 1: Calculate area for each planter
tasks.prompt:
Use your Week 2 dimension table.

Planter A: A = ___ × ___ = _____ cm²
Planter B: A = ___ × ___ = _____ cm²
Planter C: A = ___ × ___ = _____ cm²

render_hints.heading: Area Calculations
render_hints.help: Formula: A = L × W. Use your calculator.

tasks.label: Part 2: Compare
tasks.prompt:
Answer these questions using your calculations:

Which planter has the largest area? ________
Which planter has the smallest area? ________
Difference between largest and smallest: _____ cm²

render_hints.help: To find the difference, subtract the smallest area from the largest.

tasks.label: Part 3: Explain your choice
tasks.prompt:
Based on area only, which planter would you recommend to the client? Explain using your numbers.

Level B: I would recommend Planter ___ because it has an area of ___ cm², which is ___ than the others.
Level C: Write your own explanation using at least two numbers.

render_hints.help: Use the words: largest, area, cm², recommend.

embedded_supports: Formula card: A = L × W (area = length × width)
Units reminder: area is always written in cm² or m²
Sentence frame: I would recommend ___ because its area is ___ cm².
success_criteria: I used A = L × W to calculate the area of each planter.
I wrote my answer with cm².
I can say which planter has the most planting surface.

checkpoint_sheet.title: Week 3 Checkpoint — Area Check
prompts: Write the formula for area: A = ___ × ___
Calculate: L = 60 cm, W = 35 cm. Area = _____ cm²
A planter has an area of 2500 cm². Another has 3100 cm². Which is bigger? How do you know?
checkpoint_sheet.teacher_notes: Use this checkpoint to identify students who need the area formula revisited in Week 4 flex. Students showing unit errors should redo the label step.

exit_ticket.title: Week 3 Exit Check
exit_ticket.prompt: A planter is 70 cm long and 30 cm wide. What is its area? Show your work: A = ___ × ___ = _____ cm².
success_criteria: I used the formula correctly.
I wrote the answer with cm².

days.carryover_note: Flex week. Use to address gaps in area, measurement, or unit understanding before moving to volume.

content.subtitle: This week: review, catch up, or extend your area and measurement work.

slides.title: Where are we in the project?

content.scenario: We have identified dimensions and calculated areas. Next week we move to volume. This is our chance to fill any gaps.
prompts: What is area?
What formula did we use?
What is the difference between area and volume?

rows: Volume = Length × Width × Height
Volume tells us how much space is inside the planter.
Volume is measured in cubic units: cm³
Option 1: Re-measure planter dimensions and check your table.
Option 2: Redo area calculations with a different set of dimensions.
Option 3: Try the volume formula preview problem.
Option 4: Complete any missing work from Weeks 1–3.
Option 5: Create your own rectangle with a given area.

prompts: I feel confident about area: Yes / Almost / Not yet.
One thing I want to review before Week 5: ___.
One math word I can now use in a sentence: ___.

task_sheet.title: Week 4 Task Sheet — Flex Activities
instructions: Your teacher will tell you which activity to do today.
Show all work and label your units.
If you finish early, try the extension problem.

tasks.label: Activity A: Area catch-up
tasks.prompt:
Calculate the area of these two rectangles:

Rectangle 1: L = 55 cm, W = 25 cm → A = _____ cm²
Rectangle 2: L = 1.2 m, W = 0.6 m → A = _____ m²

tasks.label: Activity B: Volume preview
tasks.prompt:
A planter is 80 cm × 40 cm × 30 cm. Try to calculate its volume.

V = L × W × H = ___ × ___ × ___ = _____ cm³

What does this number represent?

render_hints.help: Think of volume as how many centimetre cubes fit inside.

embedded_supports: Formula reference: A = L × W and V = L × W × H
Units reminder: area = cm², volume = cm³
success_criteria: I completed at least one flex activity.
I used the correct formula and wrote units.
I can tell the difference between area and volume.

days.day_label: Week 5: Calculate Volume and Estimate Soil
days.carryover_note: Volume calculation and soil estimate carry forward to Week 6 materials tally.

content.subtitle: This week: calculate volume and estimate how many bags of soil we need to fill our planter.

content.task: Quick recall before we add the height dimension.
prompts: What is the formula for area?
What units do we use for area?
What is the area of our chosen planter?

rows: Volume = Length × Width × Height
Volume is measured in cubic units: cm³ or m³
Volume tells us how much soil fits inside the planter.
Calculate volume in cm³: V = L × W × H
Convert: 1000 cm³ ≈ 1 litre of soil
Each standard bag of potting soil holds about 20 litres.
Number of bags = total litres ÷ 20
Round up to the nearest whole bag (you cannot buy a half bag).

prompts: Why do we need to know the volume before we buy soil?
What happens if we buy too little soil? Too much?
Which planter design would need the most soil?

task_sheet.title: Week 5 Task Sheet — Volume and Soil Estimation
instructions: Use the formula V = L × W × H.
Show every step of your calculation.
Round up your bag estimate — always buy a little extra.

tasks.label: Part 1: Calculate volume
tasks.prompt:
Use your planter's dimensions to calculate volume:

L = ________ cm
W = ________ cm
H = ________ cm

V = ___ × ___ × ___ = __________ cm³

render_hints.heading: Volume Calculation
render_hints.help: Find your planter's dimensions on your Week 2 table.

tasks.label: Part 2: Estimate soil bags
tasks.prompt:
Convert your volume to litres and estimate bags:

Volume in cm³: __________
÷ 1000 = __________ litres
÷ 20 (litres per bag) = __________ bags
Rounded up = __________ bags

render_hints.help: Always round up — you need whole bags.

tasks.label: Part 3: Compare planters
tasks.prompt:
Would the other two planter designs need more or fewer bags of soil?

Planter A would need approximately ___ bags.
Planter B would need approximately ___ bags.
Planter C would need approximately ___ bags.

Which uses the most soil? _______

render_hints.help: Repeat the volume calculation with each planter's dimensions.

embedded_supports: Formula card: V = L × W × H
Conversion: 1000 cm³ = 1 litre; 1 bag = 20 litres
Level A: calculation steps are pre-printed — fill in the numbers only.
success_criteria: I used V = L × W × H correctly.
I converted cm³ to litres.
I estimated the number of soil bags needed.

checkpoint_sheet.title: Week 5 Checkpoint — Volume Check
prompts: Write the formula for volume: V = ___ × ___ × ___
A planter is 60 cm × 30 cm × 25 cm. Calculate V = _____ cm³
How many litres of soil does that planter need? _____ L
How many 20-litre bags? Round up: _____ bags
checkpoint_sheet.teacher_notes: Students who struggle with the conversion chain should be flagged for small-group support. The key misconception is forgetting to round up.

days.carryover_note: Materials table from Week 6 feeds into cost estimation in Week 9 and the final recommendation.

content.subtitle: This week: tally the materials needed to build and plant our planter box.

slides.title: What goes into a planter box?

content.scenario: Before we can shop for materials, we need to know exactly what we need and how many. A good materials list tells us item, quantity, and unit.
prompts: What materials does our planter need?
How do we know how many boards to buy?
Why is a tally important?

rows: Item — what the material is (lumber, screws, soil)
Quantity — how many or how much
Unit — what we are counting (boards, bags, boxes, kg)
Count each category separately — do not mix lumber and screws.
Write both a tally mark and a total number.
Check your count by counting backwards from your total.
Use the correct unit: bags of soil, not 'soils'.
Round up on quantities — you cannot use half a board.

prompts: We estimated ___ bags of soil last week. Does that match our list?
What would happen if we ran out of screws on build day?
Is it better to buy too much or too little? Why?

task_sheet.title: Week 6 Task Sheet — Materials Tally
instructions: Count each type of material from the build plan.
Record the quantity and unit in the table.
Check your soil bag estimate against your Week 5 calculation.

tasks.label: Part 1: Complete the materials table
tasks.prompt:
Fill in the materials table:

Item | Tally | Total | Unit
Lumber boards | | | boards
Corner screws | | | screws
Potting soil | | | bags
Plants or seeds | | | packets
Landscape fabric | | | pieces

render_hints.heading: Materials Tally Table
render_hints.help: Count from your build plan. Ask your teacher for the materials list if needed.

tasks.label: Part 2: Soil check
tasks.prompt:
My Week 5 soil estimate was _____ bags.
My tally today shows _____ bags.
Do they match? ______
If they are different, explain why: _______________________

render_hints.help: It is okay if they are different — explain the difference.

embedded_supports: Unit reminder: bags (soil), boards (lumber), screws (hardware)
Level A: pre-filled table with quantities to circle
Sentence frame: We need ___ ___ (unit) of ___ (item).
success_criteria: I listed all required materials with quantities and units.
I connected my tally to my soil estimate from Week 5.
I used the correct unit for each item.

exit_ticket.title: Week 6 Exit Check
exit_ticket.prompt: We need 3 boards, 24 screws, and 4 bags of soil. Which item do we need the most of? _______ How many items in total? _______
success_criteria: I identified the item with the highest quantity.
I calculated the correct total.

days.carryover_note: Growth data from Week 7 is used in the Week 9 graph and trend analysis.

content.subtitle: This week: measure your plants, record the data, and begin a line graph.

slides.title: What do we need to track?

content.scenario: Plants change over time. We can use math to track that change. We will measure plant height each week and plot the data on a line graph to see the growth pattern.
prompts: What unit should we use to measure plant height?
Why do we record measurements — not just look?
What does a line graph show that a table cannot?

slides.title: Reading and plotting a line graph

rows: The x-axis (horizontal) shows time — in our case, the week number.
The y-axis (vertical) shows height in centimetres.
Each data point is a dot. Connect the dots with a straight line.
Measure from the soil surface to the tallest leaf tip.
Record to the nearest centimetre — do not estimate mm.
If a plant has not sprouted yet, record 0 cm.
Measure the same plant each week for consistency.
Write today's date next to the measurement.

prompts: A line going upward means the plant is ___.
A flat line means the plant ___.
What would a sudden jump in height mean?

graphic_organizer.title: Plant Growth Log — Weeks 1–10
graphic_organizer.prompt: Record your plant height measurement each week. Then plot each value on the line graph below.
rows: Week 1 — Height: _____ cm — Date: _________
Week 2 — Height: _____ cm — Date: _________
Week 3 — Height: _____ cm — Date: _________
Week 4 — Height: _____ cm — Date: _________
Week 5 — Height: _____ cm — Date: _________
Week 6 — Height: _____ cm — Date: _________
Week 7 — Height: _____ cm — Date: _________
Week 8 — Height: _____ cm — Date: _________
Week 9 — Height: _____ cm — Date: _________
Week 10 — Height: _____ cm — Date: _________
success_criteria: I recorded height in cm for each week with data.
I wrote the date beside each measurement.
I plotted the data points on the graph.

exit_ticket.title: Week 7 Exit Check
exit_ticket.prompt: My plant's height this week is _____ cm. Compared to last week (_____ cm), it grew _____ cm. Is this more or less than I expected?
success_criteria: I recorded today's height with a unit.
I calculated the change from last week.

days.carryover_note: Flex week. Use to extend graph, review data accuracy, or catch up growth log entries.

content.subtitle: This week: review your growth data, extend your graph, and prepare for trend analysis.

content.scenario: Before we can analyze trends, we need clean, complete data. This week we check our growth log, fill in any gaps, and review our graph.
prompts: Do you have measurements for every week so far?
Did you measure the same plant each time?
Does your graph match your data table?

rows: Increasing trend: values go up over time.
Decreasing trend: values go down over time.
No trend (flat): values stay the same.
Option 1: Fill in any missing weeks on your growth log.
Option 2: Re-plot your graph more accurately.
Option 3: Calculate the average growth per week so far.
Option 4: Compare your plant's growth to a classmate's.
Option 5: Write one sentence describing the trend in your data.

prompts: My data is complete: Yes / No.
My graph is accurate: Yes / Needs fixing.
The trend I can see so far is: ___.

task_sheet.title: Week 8 Task Sheet — Data Review
instructions: Check your growth log is complete.
Choose one or two flex activities.
Write a one-sentence description of your data trend.

tasks.label: Activity A: Average growth
tasks.prompt:
Add up all height values recorded so far: _______ cm total
Divide by number of weeks with data: ÷ ___ weeks
Average height per week = _______ cm

What does this average tell you?

tasks.label: Activity B: Trend description
tasks.prompt:
Look at your line graph. Describe the trend in one or two sentences.

Level B: My plant's height has been ___ (increasing/decreasing/staying flat). Between Week ___ and Week ___, the growth was the fastest.
Level C: Write your own description using numbers from your graph.

render_hints.help: Use: increasing, decreasing, steepest, fastest, slowest, change.

embedded_supports: Trend vocabulary: increasing, decreasing, flat, steep, gradual, sudden
Average reminder: add all values, then divide by how many there are.
success_criteria: My growth log has data for every available week.
I can describe the trend using at least one number.
I completed at least one flex activity.

graphic_organizer.title: Growth Comparison — My Plant vs. Class Plant
rows: Week 5
Week 6
Week 7
Week 8
graphic_organizer.prompt: Compare your plant's growth to the class average for the past four weeks.
success_criteria: I recorded both sets of measurements.
I can identify which grew more each week.

days.carryover_note: Trend analysis and max/min findings from Week 9 feed directly into the Week 10 recommendation.

content.subtitle: This week: read your graph to find trends, maximums, minimums, and the biggest change.

content.task: Use your Week 7 growth log and graph.
prompts: What is the highest value on your graph?
In which week did your plant grow the most?
What trend does the overall line show?

slides.title: Reading a line graph for analysis

rows: Maximum value: the highest point on the graph.
Minimum value: the lowest point — could be 0 cm (no growth yet).
Greatest change: the steepest section of the line.
trend — the general direction of data over time
maximum — the highest recorded value
minimum — the lowest recorded value
range — maximum minus minimum
rate of change — how fast the value is changing per week

prompts: If I were to present this graph to the client, I would say: ___.
The most interesting thing about my data is ___.
One thing I would do differently next time: ___.

graphic_organizer.title: Data Analysis — Growth Summary
rows: Maximum height recorded
Minimum height recorded
Range (max − min)
Week of fastest growth
Amount of growth that week
Overall trend (describe)
graphic_organizer.prompt: Use your growth log to complete this analysis table. Write units with every measurement.
success_criteria: I identified the max and min values from my graph.
I calculated the range.
I described the overall trend in words.

task_sheet.title: Week 9 Task Sheet — Explain Your Data
instructions: Use your growth analysis table to answer these questions.
Write at least one number in every answer.
Level B: use the sentence frames provided.

tasks.label: Part 1: Describe the data
tasks.prompt:
Describe what happened to your plant over the 10 weeks.

Level B: My plant started at ___ cm and grew to a maximum of ___ cm. The biggest growth happened in Week ___.
Level C: Write a detailed description with at least three data points.

render_hints.help: Quote at least two numbers from your growth log.

tasks.label: Part 2: Recommendation setup
tasks.prompt:
Based on your data, what would you tell the client about how well the planter supported plant growth?

Level B: The planter worked ___ because the plant grew ___ cm over ___ weeks.
Level C: Write your own recommendation sentence.

render_hints.help: Sentence starter: Based on my data, I would tell the client that...

embedded_supports: Vocabulary: trend, maximum, minimum, range, rate of change
Sentence frame: My data shows that the plant grew from ___ cm to ___ cm over ___ weeks.
Level A: circle the trend description (increasing / decreasing / flat).
success_criteria: I used data from my graph in my written answer.
I quoted at least two numbers with units.
I described the overall trend in words.

days.carryover_note: Final project. Growth data, area, volume, and materials tally all converge here.

content.subtitle: This week: use your math data to write and share a justified recommendation for the client.

content.task: Look back at all your work from the project.
prompts: What was the area of our chosen planter?
How many bags of soil did we use?
What trend did the growth graph show?

rows: State your recommendation clearly: 'I recommend...' or 'Based on the data...'
Include at least one number to support your recommendation.
Explain what the number means for the client's decision.
The planter worked because the plant grew ___ cm over ___ weeks.
The area of ___ cm² was enough for ___ plants.
We used ___ bags of soil, which fit the budget.
I recommend this planter because ___.
One thing I would change is ___ because ___.

prompts: The most important number from our project is ___ because ___.
Mathematics helped us by ___.
Something I learned about measurement that I will use again: ___.

task_sheet.title: Week 10 Task Sheet — Build Your Recommendation
instructions: Gather your data from Weeks 2, 3, 5, 6, and 9.
Complete each section of the recommendation builder.
Transfer your key points to the Final Recommendation Card.

tasks.label: Part 1: Project data summary
tasks.prompt:
Record key numbers from the project:

Planter chosen: _________
Area of planting surface: _____ cm²
Volume: _____ cm³
Soil bags used: _____
Maximum plant height: _____ cm
Total growth (weeks 1–10): _____ cm

tasks.label: Part 2: Recommendation draft
tasks.prompt:
Write a short recommendation using your data:

Level B: I recommend the ___ planter because it has an area of ___ cm² and the plant grew ___ cm over the project.
Level C: Write a complete recommendation (2–3 sentences) that references both the planter design and the growth outcome.

render_hints.help: Include: planter choice, area, growth data, one reason.

embedded_supports: Sentence starters: I recommend... / Based on my data... / The plant grew...
Level A: fill in the blanks on the pre-built recommendation sentence.
Data reference: pull numbers from Weeks 2, 3, 5, 6, and 9.
success_criteria: I included at least two numbers in my recommendation.
I named the planter I recommend.
I explained what the numbers mean for the client.

final_response_sheet.title: Final Recommendation Card — Math 8
final_response_sheet.prompt: Based on your data from this project, write your final recommendation to the client. Include at least two measurements.
success_criteria: I stated which planter I recommend.
I used at least two measurements (area, volume, or growth height).
I explained what the numbers mean for the client's decision.
embedded_supports: Level A: circle: I recommend Planter ___. It has an area of ___ cm² and the plant grew ___ cm.
Level B: I recommend the ___ planter. Its area is ___ cm². The plant grew ___ cm.
Level C: Write your own formal recommendation with at least two pieces of evidence.

checkpoint_sheet.title: Week 10 Checkpoint — Final Evidence Check
prompts: Student included at least two measurements in their recommendation: Y / N
Calculations are accurate and units are labeled: Y / N
Student can explain what their recommendation means for the client: Y / N
Conference note (optional):
checkpoint_sheet.teacher_notes: This is the summative assessment moment. Oral explanation is valid evidence for all students. The final recommendation card is the primary artifact.
